stories.title: Quantum sensors turn weird physics into measurement tools
stories.desc: Quantum states are fragile, but that fragility can be useful. Tiny changes in gravity, time, motion, or magnetic fields can disturb them in measurable ways.

stories.title: Metamaterials shape waves with tiny structures
stories.desc: Some materials are designed with patterns smaller than the waves they control. This can let scientists steer light, sound, or microwaves in unusual ways.

stories.title: Particle detectors work like giant mystery cameras
stories.desc: Particle physicists often do not see a particle directly. They see tracks, flashes, and energy deposits, then reconstruct the hidden event.

stories.title: Gravity can turn galaxies into natural telescopes
stories.desc: Massive galaxies can bend light from objects behind them. This gravitational lensing can magnify distant galaxies and reveal hidden structure.

stories.title: Prime numbers are simple to define and hard to predict
stories.desc: A prime number has only two whole-number divisors: 1 and itself. That tiny rule opens into some of the deepest patterns in mathematics.

stories.title: A good experiment removes wrong answers
stories.desc: Science often works like detective logic. A strong observation does not only support one idea; it rules out many weaker explanations.

stories.title: Exoplanet atmospheres leave color fingerprints
stories.desc: When starlight passes through a planet's atmosphere, molecules can absorb special colors. Those missing colors become clues about distant worlds.

stories.title: Physics-informed AI learns with rules, not only examples
stories.desc: Some AI models are trained with scientific constraints. The goal is to make predictions that fit data while staying physically plausible.

stories.title: Wavelength decides what a wave can notice
stories.desc: A wave interacts strongly with objects near its own scale. That is why radar, infrared, visible light, and X-rays reveal different things.
